title: He Kākano Ahau: I Am a Seed - Understanding Our Place in the Mangakōtukutuku Stream Community
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: This unit is centered around Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. Students will explore how this awa has shaped our community identity, supported traditional Māori food gathering practices (mahinga kai), and serves as a living connection to our Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors. The unit examines contemporary challenges facing the stream while honoring its role as a taonga tuku iho (treasure handed down).
learningObjectives: Analyze how Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku has influenced community development and cultural practices over time
Investigate and document current environmental challenges facing the awa using both mātauranga Māori and scientific approaches
Collaborate with local kaumātua and community groups to develop practical solutions for stream restoration
Create and present a digital story that demonstrates understanding of our collective kaitiakitanga responsibilities

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Awa
activities.description: Students begin by mapping the historical significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, identifying traditional pā sites, mahinga kai areas, and significant landmarks along its banks. They'll interview local kaumātua to record oral histories and create a collaborative digital timeline showing how the awa has shaped our community identity.

activities.title: Matauranga Māori Meets Science
activities.description: Students conduct water quality testing at three points along the stream while simultaneously learning traditional Māori methods for assessing stream health. They'll compare scientific data with mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of native species, water clarity, plant health) to develop a holistic understanding of the awa's current state.

activities.title: Kaitiaki in Action
activities.description: Working with local environmental group 'Friends of Mangakōtukutuku Stream,' students participate in a hands-on restoration project. This may include planting native riparian vegetation, removing invasive species, or creating habitats for native tuna (eels) and kōura (crayfish).

activities.title: Community Voice Project
activities.description: Students design and conduct interviews with diverse community members (farmers, local iwi representatives, residents, business owners) to understand different perspectives on the stream's importance and challenges. They'll use this research to identify common values and potential conflicts.

activities.title: He Kākano Ahau Exhibition
activities.description: Students create multimedia presentations (digital stories, interactive maps, or physical displays) that showcase their learning journey and propose specific, actionable solutions for ongoing kaitiakitanga of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, to be presented to whānau and community stakeholders.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and knowledge holders from local marae
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' monitoring data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Digital mapping tools (ArcGIS Online or Google Earth with historical layers)
Water quality testing kits and traditional Māori environmental assessment frameworks

tasks: Create a detailed digital story map showing historical and contemporary significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Produce a comprehensive water quality report comparing scientific and mātauranga Māori assessment methods
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan with specific, achievable recommendations for stream restoration

nzcAlignment: Identity, Culture, and Organization: Students learn about the ways in which different groups in their community have organized themselves to manage resources
Place and Environment: Students understand how people interact with natural and cultural environments and the consequences of these interactions
Continuity and Change: Students understand how people's management of resources impacts sustainability and intergenerational equity
The Arts in Social Contexts: Students use digital technologies to communicate their findings and recommendations effectively
